nativeRange.habitat: Large river channels, river margins, flooded forests (igapó/várzea), and deep pools; found over sand, mud, and leaf-litter substrates in major river systems including the Amazon, Orinoco, and their tributaries

decorPreferences: large smooth boulders or rounded rocks (no sharp edges for scaleless skin)
heavy-duty sand or fine gravel substrate
minimal decor — adults destroy everything not fixed down
extremely robust external filtration (high turnover rate required)

diet.notes: Apex predator. Wild diet includes live fish, crustaceans, fallen fruits, and carrion. In captivity accepts large meaty foods: whole fish (smelt, tilapia, lance fish), prawns, mussels, earthworms, and large sinking carnivore pellets or sticks. Juveniles sold at ~10 cm will consume feeder fish, prawns, and meaty wet/frozen foods readily. Feeds at night; will actively hunt and consume any tankmate small enough to engulf — including fish up to roughly half its own body length. Freshwater mussels and other shellfish help satisfy natural foraging behavior. Do not feed fatty freshwater fish (goldfish, rosy reds) long-term due to thiaminase and fat content.

goodWith: other very large (>80 cm) robust fish only in public-aquarium settings with expert management
large Arapaima, large Pacu, and large Pseudoplatystoma species in species-appropriate public exhibits — staff supervision required
avoidWith: any fish that fits in its mouth (will be eaten)
other red-tailed catfish (extreme intraspecific aggression; injuries and fatalities common)
medium or small cichlids, tetras, or any community fish
any tankmate in a private home aquarium — unsuitable for hobby keeping entirely

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Phractocephalus hemioliopterus. Shows characteristic red/orange caudal fin and dark dorsal coloration against cream/white ventral. Verify availability before use.
Wikimedia Commons image showing adult specimen. Good lateral view of full body. Confirm current license status before deployment.

summary: A massive Amazonian apex predator reaching 130–180 cm in the wild. The red-tailed catfish is wholly unsuitable for the home aquarium; it requires 5,000 L+ with public-aquarium-grade filtration. Sold cheaply as 10 cm juveniles, it rapidly outgrows any private tank and cannot be re-homed. Do not purchase.
careNotes:
The red-tailed catfish (Phractocephalus hemioliopterus) is one of the most commonly impulse-purchased and subsequently problem-rehomed fish in the freshwater hobby. Juveniles are sold at roughly 10 cm for as little as $15–20 USD and grow at a rate of approximately 5–8 cm per month under good conditions, reaching 40–50 cm within the first year. Wild specimens reach 130–180 cm and exceed 80 kg. No private home aquarium is realistically suitable for an adult: the minimum credible tank size is 5,000 L (approximately 1,300 US gallons) with a footprint of at least 5 m in length, and filtration must turn over the full tank volume 8–10 times per hour. Even public aquaria with professional life-support systems find this species demanding.

The red-tailed catfish is scaleless — it lacks the protective bony scute layer of armored catfishes — making it highly sensitive to copper-based medications (ich treatments, most anti-parasitic dips), salt, and other copper-containing compounds standard in fishkeeping. Scaleless skin is also easily abraded; sharp decor, netting, and rough substrate cause wounds that rapidly become infected.

This species is an apex ambush predator. Any fish small enough to fit in its mouth will be consumed, including tankmates added simultaneously at a smaller size. It has been documented consuming fish up to roughly 50% of its own body length. Aggressive territorial behavior toward conspecifics makes group keeping impractical even at public-aquarium scale.

Fishkeeping media including Practical Fishkeeping and Aquarium Co-Op have published explicit "do not buy" advisories for this species. It is placed in the same category of grossly oversold, unsuitable fish as the Pacu (Colossoma macropomum), Clown Knife Fish (Chitala chitala), and Iridescent Shark (Pangasianodon hypophthalmus). Re-homing adult specimens is extremely difficult; public aquaria and zoos routinely decline donations, and the fish cannot be released into local waterways without serious ecological risk. Euthanasia of an adult specimen is the reality faced by many hobbyists who purchased a juvenile without researching adult size.

If you wish to keep large Amazonian catfish in a serious large-tank setup, consider the Sailfin Pleco (Pterygoplichthys), Ripsaw Catfish (Oxydoras niger), or Tiger Shovelnose Catfish (Pseudoplatystoma tigrinum) — all of which have more manageable adult sizes and are far more commonly kept successfully in private large-format systems.
breedingNotes: No documented captive breeding. Spawning has not been achieved in hobby or public-aquarium settings; reproductive biology in captivity is not established. In the wild, spawning is believed to occur seasonally during the flood season (high-water period) in flooded forest zones, but details remain poorly described in the scientific literature. Captive breeding is considered not practically achievable outside of very large zoological facilities.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Phractocephalus hemioliopterus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 134 cm in captivity, reported to 180 cm in wild), temperature 20–26°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 2–15 dH, native range (Amazon and Orinoco basins), habitat notes, diet (piscivore/carnivore), water parameter requirements, and scaleless copper sensitivity warning. Tank minimum extrapolated from SeriouslyFish size data per playbook guidance (5000 L minimum for public-aquarium-only classification).

additional.name: FishBase — Phractocephalus hemioliopterus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Pimelodidae, Siluriformes), native range (Amazon, Orinoco basins; Brazil, Colombia, Venezuela, Peru, Ecuador, Bolivia, Guyana), adult size (reported to 134.5 cm TL, max weight 80 kg), temperature range, and lifespan data. Confirmed carnivorous diet classification.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Red-Tailed Catfish
additional.notes: Trade perspective. Confirmed 'do not buy' advisory for home aquarists; noted specimens sold at 10 cm grow to unmanageable size rapidly. Highlighted copper sensitivity, predatory behavior, and re-homing difficulty. Temperature and pH ranges consistent with SeriouslyFish primary source.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Red-tailed catfish: don't buy this fish
additional.notes: Strong 'do not buy' editorial. Cited routine abandonment, inability to rehome, and rapid juvenile-to-adult size escalation as primary concerns. Confirmed scaleless copper sensitivity and minimum tank requirements beyond home capability. Placed alongside Pacu, Clown Knife, and Pangasius in the same unsuitable-for-hobby category.

additional.name: Seriously Fish — care notes cross-check (hobbyist unsuitable classification)
additional.notes: FishBase food items data confirming piscivore/carnivore classification for Phractocephalus hemioliopterus (FishBase species ID 13418). Diet includes fish, crustaceans, and plant matter (fruits). Supports carnivore diet type and large-prey feeding notes.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding documented. Spawning conditions, courtship, and reproductive biology in captivity are not established. Wild spawning believed seasonal during flood-season in flooded forest zones.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches 130–180 cm in wild; needs 5000 L+ public-aquarium-scale enclosure. Sold tiny but rapidly outgrows every home setup.
